title: WTD
text:
Introduction
Quick exploration of INPA literature on water table depth (WTD) to build a small project for a 2 month mobility.

title: Ducke
text:
Code
library(tidyverse)
env <- readxl::read_xlsx("data/raw_data/ducke/Species Weighted Averages.xlsx", 2) %>% 
  select(Especie, hand.mean) %>% 
  rename(species = Especie, hand = hand.mean) %>% 
  separate(species, c("genus", "species")) %>% 
  filter(!(species %in% c("na", "indet")))
ducke <- readxl::read_xlsx("data/raw_data/ducke/ducke_arvores_20140214_corr_pom_planilha de trabalho.xlsx") %>% 
  mutate(dbh = as.numeric(dap3c)) %>% 
  select(especie,   numero, parcela,    x,  y,  lado, dbh) %>% 
  rename(species = especie, id = numero, plot = parcela, side = lado) %>% 
  separate(species, c("genus", "species")) %>% 
  filter(!(species %in% c("na", "indet"))) %>% 
  filter(dbh > 10) %>% 
  mutate(group = ifelse(dbh > 30, "large", "intermediate")) %>% 
  mutate(area = ifelse(dbh > 30, 0.5, 1)) %>% 
  left_join(env)
ducke_sum <- ducke %>% 
  group_by(group) %>% 
  mutate(n_plot = length(unique(plot))) %>% 
  group_by(group, genus, species) %>% 
  summarise(hand = mean(hand, na.rm = TRUE), 
            abundance = n()) %>% 
  mutate(abundance = ifelse(group == "intermediate", 
                            abundance*2, abundance)) %>% 
  group_by(genus, species) %>% 
  summarise(hand = median(hand, na.rm = TRUE),
            sd_abundance = sd(abundance, na.rm = TRUE),
            abundance = mean(abundance, na.rm = TRUE)/72)
write_tsv(ducke_sum, "data/derived_data/ducke_sum.tsv")


For trees above 10-cm DBH, Ducke has 27 0.5-ha plots for trees in 10-30-cm DBH and 27 1-ha plots for trees >30-cm DBH. We computed then the mean abundance across all 72 plots and the associated mean Height Above Nearest Drainage (HAND, m).


Code
library(tidyverse)
read_tsv("data/derived_data/ducke_sum.tsv") %>% 
  ggplot(aes(hand, abundance)) +
  geom_point() +
  theme_bw() +
  scale_y_log10() +
  xlab("Mean HAND [m]") +
  ylab("Mean Abundance [N/ha]")





Species mean abundance (N/Ha) versus mean HAND (m) in Ducke.

title: Paracou
text:
Code
library(tidyverse)
library(sf)
library(terra)
paracou <- read_tsv("data/raw_data/paracou/paracou.tsv") %>% 
  filter(Forest == "Paracou") %>% 
  filter(as.numeric(Plot) %in% 1:15) %>% 
  group_by(Plot) %>% 
  filter(CensusYear == max(CensusYear)) %>% 
  select(Plot, Family, Genus, Species, idTree, Xutm, Yutm) %>% 
  rename(plot = Plot, family = Family, genus = Genus, species = Species,
         id = idTree, x = Xutm, y = Yutm) %>%  
  filter(!is.na(x), !is.na(y), species != "Indet.")
xy <- paracou %>% 
  st_as_sf(coords = c("x", "y"), crs = 32622)
hand <- rast("data/raw_data/paracou/RelativeElevation_1m.tif")
crs(hand) <- crs(xy)
paracou$hand <- extract(hand, xy)$RelativeElevation_1m
paracou_sum <- paracou %>% 
  group_by(family, genus, species) %>% 
  summarise(hand = median(hand, na.rm = TRUE),
            abundance = n()/(6.25*15))
write_tsv(paracou_sum, "data/derived_data/paracou_sum.tsv")


For trees above 10-cm DBH, I used plots 1 to 15 that are all 6.25-ha and computed then the mean abundance across all 15 plots and the associated mean Height Above Nearest Drainage (HAND, m).


Code
library(tidyverse)
read_tsv("data/derived_data/paracou_sum.tsv") %>% 
  ggplot(aes(hand, abundance)) +
  geom_point() +
  theme_bw() +
  scale_y_log10() +
  xlab("Mean HAND [m]") +
  ylab("Mean Abundance [ N/ha]")





Species mean abundance (N/Ha) versus mean HAND (m) in Paracou.

title: Species
text:
We used the World Flora Online backbone to homogenise taxonomy across sites with a strict rule keeping only unique single match.


Code
library(tidyverse)
library(WorldFlora)
library(fuzzyjoin)
taxo <- list(
  Ducke = "data/derived_data/ducke_sum.tsv",
  Paracou = "data/derived_data/paracou_sum.tsv"
) %>% lapply(read_tsv) %>% 
  bind_rows(.id = "site") %>% 
  select(family, genus, species) %>% 
  unique() %>% 
  mutate(scientific = paste(genus, species)) %>% 
  arrange(scientific)
wfo <- read_tsv("data/raw_data/WFO_Backbone.zip")
wfo_sub <- wfo %>% filter(family %in% taxo$family)
write_tsv(wfo_sub, "data/derived_data/backbone.tsv")
cleaned <- WFO.match.fuzzyjoin(
    spec.data = taxo$scientific,
    WFO.file = "data/derived_data/backbone.tsv"
  )
write_tsv(cleaned, "data/derived_data/wfo_res.tsv")
read_tsv("data/derived_data/wfo_res.tsv") %>% 
  filter(Matched, Unique) %>% 
  select(spec.name, family, genus, specificEpithet, scientificName) %>% 
  rename(
    scientific = spec.name,
    family_cleaned = family,
    genus_cleaned = genus,
    species_cleaned = specificEpithet,
    scientific_cleaned = scientificName
  ) %>% 
  write_tsv("data/derived_data/cor_tab.tsv")




Code
library(tidyverse)
read_tsv("data/derived_data/cor_tab.tsv") %>% 
  head() %>% 
  knitr::kable(caption = "Updated taxonomy extract.")

title: Comparisons
text:
Shared species
Ducke has a total of 948 species after quick taxonomic correction against 706 Paracou of which only 223 (20%) are shared.


Code
library(ggvenn)
ggvenn(list('Ducke (72-ha)' = ducke$scientific, 
            "Paracou (93.75-ha)" = paracou$scientific))





Unique and shared species across sites

t <- bind_rows(
  head(na.omit(data)),

)

title: Comparisons
text:
Abundances
Abundances are slightly correlated across sites.


Code
data %>% 
  mutate(label = ifelse((genus %in% t$genus && species %in% t$species),
                        paste0(str_sub(genus, 1, 1), ". ", species), "")) %>% 
  ggplot(aes(abundance_Paracou, abundance_Ducke, col = hand_mean)) +
  geom_abline() +
  geom_point() +
  ggrepel::geom_text_repel(aes(label = label)) +
  theme_bw() +
  scale_x_log10() +
  scale_y_log10() +
  scale_color_viridis_c() +
  scale_color_viridis_c("Mean\nHAND\n[m]") +
  ggpubr::stat_cor() +
  xlab("Paracou Mean Abundance [N/ha]") +
  ylab("Ducke Mean Abundance [N/ha]")





Abundances (N/ha) comparisons for the common species between Ducke and Paracou.

title: Comparisons
text:
Habitats
Mean HAND does not seem correlated across sites, but this probably a too quick and dirty measure of habitat.


Code
m_hand_duc <- median(data$hand_Ducke, na.rm = TRUE)
m_hand_par <- median(data$hand_Paracou, na.rm = TRUE)
data %>% 
  mutate(label = ifelse((genus %in% t$genus && species %in% t$species),
                        paste0(str_sub(genus, 1, 1), ". ", species), "")) %>% 
  ggplot(aes(hand_Paracou, hand_Ducke, col = abundance_mean)) +
  geom_abline() +
  geom_point() +
  ggrepel::geom_text_repel(aes(label = label)) +
  theme_bw() +
  scale_color_viridis_c("Mean\nAbundance\n[N/ha]", trans = "log") +
  ggpubr::stat_cor() +
  xlab("Paracou Mean HAND [HAND, m]") +
  ylab("Ducke Mean HAND [m]") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = m_hand_par, linetype = "dashed") + 
  geom_hline(yintercept = m_hand_duc, linetype = "dashed")





HAND (m) comparisons for the common species between Ducke and Paracou.




57 species are common between Paracou and Ducke with low HAND values and 57 with high HAND values.


Code
ggvenn(list("Ducke HAND-" = filter(ducke, hand < m_hand_duc)$scientific, 
            "Paracou HAND-" = filter(paracou, hand < m_hand_par)$scientific, 
            "Ducke HAND+" = filter(ducke, hand >= m_hand_duc)$scientific, 
            "Paracou HAND+" = filter(paracou, hand >= m_hand_par)$scientific))





Shared species across sites and high or low values of HAND (above or below the site median).






Code
data %>% 
  na.omit() %>% 
  filter(hand_Paracou > m_hand_par, hand_Ducke > m_hand_duc) %>% 
  head() %>% 
  knitr::kable(caption = "Common species between Paracou and Ducke with high HAND values.")

title: Literature
section: Flavia R. C. Costa et al. (2022)
text:
Flavia R. C. Costa et al. (2022)
Abstract:

Tropical forest function determined by water availability
Groundwater related to soil water through the water table depth (WT)
Shallow WT forests (WT < 5 m) underrepresented in research
Shallow WT ~50% Amazon & different response CC droughts
Review WT patterns and consequences + conceptual model (env, traits, CC)
Shallow WT distinct composition: resource-acquisitive, hydrologically vulnerable, shorter canopies and lower biomass.
‘normal’ years shallow WT forests have higher mortality and lower productivity
moderate droughts mortality is buffered and productivity increases
severe drought more sensitive due to shallow roots and drought-intolerant traits?

Notes:

lack of WT variations in Dynamic Vegetation Models
Forest dyn | CC ~ f( WT x Clim x FT)
To sim Delta WT TROLL needs stomatal control improvement that we plan to dev for rainfall exclusion experiment
Shallow WT results in shallower roots due to hypoxia or anoxia (to dev)
Low runoff in the Amazon with ref
Wt driven by topo x clim
Capillary water uprise can go from <1m in sand to few metres in clay
Anoxic conditions > from root respiration to alcoholic fermentation > 1 vs 36 ATP prod per glycose > limit root growth > reduce root permeability > reduce stomatal conductance > reduce photosysnthesis
Anoxic conditions > reduce available nitrogen
Shallower roots > higher uprooting
Agenda: (1) understanding shallow WT productivity shifts, (2) explore a potential tipping point, (3) link WTD, WUE, CO2 enrichment and phosphorous availability, (4) predict forest responses to CC with field and remote sensing data

title: Literature
text:
Aleixo et al. (2019)
Abstract:

Increasing mortality in tropical moist forests
Importance of climate and speceis traits evaluated with survival >1,000 trees, >200 species, >50year in the Central Amazon
Drought, heat, storms and extreme rainy years increase mortality to at least two years
Pioneers, softwoods and evergreens are especially sensitive
This may result in faster carbon sequestration but lower carbon storage

title: Literature
text:
Cosme et al. (2017)
Abstract:

local filtering of species link to hydraulic architectural traits?
hydrological microhabitat partitioning?
hydraulic characteristics at tissue (anatomical traits, wood specific gravity (WSG)), organ (leaf area, specific leaf area (SLA), leaf area : sapwood area ratio) and whole-plant (height) levels for 28 pairs of congeneric species specialized in valleys or plateaus
On plateaus, higher WSG, lower mean vessel area, mean vessel hydraulic diameter, sapwood area and SLA; traits commonly associated with hydraulic safety
2 strategies for either efficiency or safety were detected, based on vessel size or allocation to sapwood

title: Literature
section: Flávia Regina Capellotto Costa et al. (2020)
text:
Flávia Regina Capellotto Costa et al. (2020)
Abstract:

20-year monitoring of 6 biological groups over 100 km2 at Ducke
decrease in abundance (9 – 35 %) and richness (8 – 25 %) along time, with some taxonomic and functional reorganization
more extensive changes in the valleys for trees and ants, and in first-order streams for fish
bird and small trees had different patterns of composition change in valleys and plateaus
forest and streams are becoming more dynamic as the hydrological cycle intensifies due to climate changes
suggest riparian areas in the valleys are hydrological refuges during droughts

title: Literature
text:
Castilho et al. (2006)
Abstract:

72 1-ha plots tree and palm AGB ~ soil gradients (texture & nutrients) and topography (altitude & slope)
8% to 98% sand, slopes 0.5° to 27°, altitude range 70m
AGB (palm+trees>1cm) 327.8 Mg/ha (210.9-426.3)
two-fold variation among plots, soil or topography explaining 20%
AGB ~ +%clay +altitude !=slope
slope explained 14% of the AGB in understory & emergent

title: Literature
text:
Castilho et al. (2010)
Abstract:

72 1-ha plots over 2-yr: soil & topographic ~ AGB change
AGB increased over 2-yr 1.65 Mg/ha/yr (1.15-2.79)
understory & sub-canopy higher rates
rate != soil or topography but ~ year of measurement

title: Literature
text:
Esteban et al. (2020)
Abstract:

Demography 30 abundant tree species >20years, impacts of droughts & extreme wet periods, and topograpy (shallow to deep WT) with functional traits on climate effects
Dry & wet extremes ~ -growth +mortality. But higher impact of interaction.
Despite more drought-tolerant, species deep WT were more negatively affected than hydraulically vulnerable species shallow WT.
Interaction of wet–dry extremes and WT depth modulated tree responses to climate, providing buffers to droughts in Amazonia.

title: Literature
text:
Garcia et al. (2021)
Abstract:

ITV LMA, WD, xylem embolism resistance 2 dominant species along water and light availability accounting tree age and height.
ITV hydraulic ~ community variation
ITV hydraulic ~ hydrological env, +embolism resistance in deep than shallow WT
ITV LMA & WD ~ age, height, light
uncoupled coordination among carbon acquisition/conservation and water-use traits

title: Literature
text:
Toledo et al. (2016)
Abstract:

soil, species composition, and trait distribution among 72 1-ha plots with fixed climate and control soil with plots design
Soil => composition shift => CWM WD
(40% AGB, 24% mortality, 18% coarse wood production) ~ clay, CEC, CWM WD, NMDS1
Lower biomass and higher mortality on sandy soils is a consequence of dominance of species with low to medium wood density adapted to waterlogged and nutrient-poor sandy soils.
Mesoscale variation in biomass and dynamics is caused by co-occurrence of species with similar traits in different parts of the edaphic gradient





Aleixo, Izabela, Darren Norris, Lia Hemerik, Antenor Barbosa, Eduardo Prata, Flávia Costa, and Lourens Poorter. 2019. “Amazonian Rainforest Tree Mortality Driven by Climate and Functional Traits.” Nature Climate Change 9 (5): 384–88. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41558-019-0458-0.


Castilho, Carolina V. de, William E. Magnusson, R. Nazaré O. de Araújo, and Flávio J. Luizão. 2010. “Short-Term Temporal Changes in Tree Live Biomass in a Central Amazonian Forest, Brazil.” Biotropica 42 (1): 95–103. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1744-7429.2009.00543.x.


Castilho, Carolina V. de, William E. Magnusson, R. Nazaré O. de Araújo, Regina C. C. Luizão, Flávio J. Luizão, Albertina P. Lima, and Niro Higuchi. 2006. “Variation in Aboveground Tree Live Biomass in a Central Amazonian Forest: Effects of Soil and Topography.” Forest Ecology and Management 234 (1-3): 85–96. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foreco.2006.06.024.


Cosme, Luiza H. M., Juliana Schietti, Flávia R. C. Costa, and Rafael S. Oliveira. 2017. “The Importance of Hydraulic Architecture to the Distribution Patterns of Trees in a Central Amazonian Forest.” New Phytologist 215 (1): 113–25. https://doi.org/10.1111/nph.14508.


Costa, Flavia R. C., Juliana Schietti, Scott C. Stark, and Marielle N. Smith. 2022. “The Other Side of Tropical Forest Drought: Do Shallow Water Table Regions of Amazonia Act as Large-Scale Hydrological Refugia from Drought?” New Phytologist 237 (3): 714–33. https://doi.org/10.1111/nph.17914.


Costa, Flávia Regina Capellotto, Jansen Alfredo Sampaio Zuanon, Fabricio Beggiato Baccaro, Juliana Schietti de Almeida, Juliana da Silva Menger, Jorge Luiz Pereira Souza, Gabriel Costa Borba, et al. 2020. “EFFECTS OF CLIMATE CHANGE ON CENTRAL AMAZONIAN FORESTS: A TWO DECADES SYNTHESIS OF MONITORING TROPICAL BIODIVERSITY.” Oecologia Australis 24 (02): 317–35. https://doi.org/10.4257/oeco.2020.2402.07.


Esteban, Erick J. L., Carolina V. Castilho, Karina L. Melgaço, and Flávia R. C. Costa. 2020. “The Other Side of Droughts: Wet Extremes and Topography as Buffers of Negative Drought Effects in an Amazonian Forest.” New Phytologist 229 (4): 1995–2006. https://doi.org/10.1111/nph.17005.


Garcia, Maquelle N, Jia Hu, Tomas F Domingues, Peter Groenendijk, Rafael S Oliveira, and Flávia R C Costa. 2021. “Local Hydrological Gradients Structure High Intraspecific Variability in Plant Hydraulic Traits in Two Dominant Central Amazonian Tree Species.” Edited by Howard Griffiths. Journal of Experimental Botany 73 (3): 939–52. https://doi.org/10.1093/jxb/erab432.


Toledo, José Julio, Carolina V. Castilho, William E. Magnusson, and Henrique E. M. Nascimento. 2016. “Soil Controls Biomass and Dynamics of an Amazonian Forest Through the Shifting of Species and Traits.” Brazilian Journal of Botany 40 (2): 451–61. https://doi.org/10.1007/s40415-016-0351-2.
